title: Spanish Garlic Prawns

subs.name: Button mushrooms or paneer cubes follow the same method for a vegetarian version; cook mushrooms a minute or two longer until they caramelise
Indian dried red chillies (byadgi or guntur variety) are a direct substitute; remove seeds for less heat. A pinch of red chilli flakes also works
The olive oil quantity here is small enough that using it is worthwhile; in a pinch, refined sunflower oil with a small spoonful of butter gives a similar richness
Fresh coriander leaves (dhania) are a good Indian substitute; the flavour is different but fresh and green in the same spirit

steps.text: Pat the prawns very dry with paper towels — any surface moisture will cause the oil to splatter and can water down the sauce. Season them lightly with salt. Slice the garlic as thin as possible with a sharp knife so the slices turn golden rather than burn.

Pour the olive oil into a wide frying pan or kadai and add the garlic slices and dried red chillies while the oil is still cold. Set the heat to medium and let everything warm up together for 3–4 minutes — the garlic will sizzle gently and turn pale gold at the edges. This slow infusion is key to the flavour of the dish.

When the garlic is lightly golden (not brown), add the prawns in a single layer, spreading them out so each one makes full contact with the hot oil. Cook for 90 seconds without touching them, then flip each prawn and cook for another 60–90 seconds. The prawns are done the moment they turn pink and curl into a loose C-shape — they should not curl tightly, which means they are overcooked.

Take the pan off the heat immediately and squeeze the lemon juice over the prawns. Scatter the parsley generously on top and stir once to coat everything. Serve right away — ideally in the pan itself or a warmed bowl — with bread or rice to soak up the fragrant oil.

safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Cook seafood until safely done for the species and preparation; keep it chilled before cooking.

substitution_notes: prawn → Button mushrooms or paneer cubes follow the same method for a vegetarian version; cook mushrooms a minute or two longer until they caramelise; dried-red-chilli → Indian dried red chillies (byadgi or guntur variety) are a direct substitute; remove seeds for less heat. A pinch of red chilli flakes also works; olive-oil → The olive oil quantity here is small enough that using it is worthwhile; in a pinch, refined sunflower oil with a small spoonful of butter gives a similar richness; parsley → Fresh coriander leaves (dhania) are a good Indian substitute; the flavour is different but fresh and green in the same spirit
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A standard kadai or non-stick frying pan works perfectly here. Indian dried red chillies (available at any market) are an exact substitute — byadgi chillies add colour, guntur add heat. Fresh tiger prawns from any fish market or supermarket are ideal; frozen prawns work too but must be fully thawed and thoroughly dried first. The key Indian grocery item to pick up is good quality extra virgin olive oil, available at big supermarkets — the quantity used is small so it is worth using the real thing for flavour.
